editableNewDialogue.text: Quick question: if I see “iodate” and “iodide” used kind of interchangeably in a paper, is that just sloppy writing or can it be context-dependent?

Usually sloppy writing. “Iodide” is I−, while “iodate” is IO3− (different oxidation state, different reactivity). They’re not interchangeable, and swapping the terms can seriously change what chemistry you’d expect (e.g., redox behavior). If you’re unsure, check whether the reported formula has oxygen in the anion, or whether they mention an oxidizer/reduction step—iodate is an oxidizer; iodide is typically a reductant or spectator anion.
So if someone wanted a tetraethylammonium iodate, is that even a normal salt you can have, like TEA+ IO3−?

Yes, tetraethylammonium iodate is a reasonable-sounding ionic salt on paper: (C2H5)4N+ paired with IO3−. Whether it’s commonly used is a different question—iodate brings oxidizing behavior and different solubility compared with halides like I−/Br−/Cl−.

As a generic lab-prep concept (not a procedure): you’d typically combine a tetraethylammonium source (often the hydroxide or a halide salt) with an iodate source (like an alkali-metal iodate) to swap counterions, then isolate the tetraethylammonium iodate if it has favorable solubility/crystallization.
If you were trying to pick a counterion just to be “non-interfering,” what are the usual go-to anions?

Common “less coordinating / less interfering” choices (context-dependent) include perchlorate (ClO4−), hexafluorophosphate (PF6−), tetrafluoroborate (BF4−), and sometimes triflate (OTf−). Each has caveats: perchlorates can be hazardous with organics; PF6− can hydrolyze under harsh conditions; BF4− can decompose under strong base. In water-based intercalation/exfoliation chemistry, people often still use simpler anions (Cl−, NO3−, acetate) because they’re cheap and soluble, even if they’re not perfectly “inert.”
Unrelated, but I keep seeing old journal PDFs where µ gets mangled into weird characters. Is that just encoding?

Yep—mostly text encoding and font substitution issues. The micro symbol “µ” can get converted into “u”, a Greek letter sequence, or even a blank box when PDFs are OCR’d or copied between systems. It’s especially common in older scans or when a PDF is generated with a non-embedded symbol font.

if i go the iodide route (like TEAI) for swapping into sodium titanate, does it usually end up messing with the UV/vis because iodide likes to get oxidized? i’m trying to keep the optical part clean, not accidentally build an I2/IO3- side show

It can, yes. I− is comparatively easy to oxidize under oxidative/photocatalytic conditions, so depending on light intensity, dissolved O2, and any photoactive phases present, you can see iodine/tri‑iodide signatures that complicate UV/vis. If your goal is “optically quiet” intercalation/exfoliation, people often try to (1) work oxygen‑poor/in the dark during the ion-exchange step, (2) wash thoroughly and monitor the supernatant for I3−, and/or (3) choose a counter‑ion that’s less redox-active if the optical readout is the priority.
small correction btw — i’m not *anti-bromine* in general. i just don’t like handling bromide alkylating reagents (ethyl bromide etc). bromide salts like TEABr/TBABr are fine if they’re already made.

Got it — so it’s not a blanket “no bromine-containing compounds” preference. It’s specifically avoiding brominated alkylating agents during synthesis/handling, while being okay with pre-made bromide salts (e.g., TEABr, TBABr) if needed.
